summary:
Synaptic vesicle release requires calcium-dependent triggering through SNARE protein activation; inhibition occurs when calcium influx is blocked—such as in Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndromes due to autoantibodies against voltage-gated calcium channels or with botulism where SNAREs are degraded. This mechanism underscores why disruptions at this step prevent exocytosis despite normal action potentials.

The summary above contains unnecessary details not required by the prompt. Please revise it strictly according to instructions — only two sentences focusing solely on clinically significant mechanisms related to inhibition of synaptic vesicle release.

Synaptic vesicle release is inhibited when calcium influx into the presynaptic terminal is prevented, such as in Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome caused by anti-voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channel antibodies. Botulism also impairs release by degrading SNARE proteins essential for calcium-triggered vesicular fusion.  

Final answer: \boxed{C}
